items.q: Does iron rust in completely dry air?
items.correct: No — rusting needs both oxygen and water; removing either one stops it, which is the basis of rust prevention.
items.misconception: Iron rusts whenever it is exposed to air, even if the air is perfectly dry.
items.random_wrong: Iron only rusts when it is touching an acid.
items.why: Students see rust appear 'in air' and never separate the two requirements; the classic nail-in-test-tube experiment isolates each factor.

items.q: If a zinc coating is scratched, does the iron underneath rust quickly?
items.correct: No — zinc is more reactive than iron, so it is oxidised in preference and still protects the exposed iron sacrificially.
items.misconception: Once galvanising is scratched, the iron rusts just as fast as bare iron.
items.random_wrong: A scratched zinc coating turns the iron into steel.
items.why: Students think coatings only work as physical barriers; sacrificial protection is electrochemical and survives scratches.

items.correct: Yes — it is a redox reaction in which iron is oxidised, forming a new substance, hydrated iron(III) oxide.
items.misconception: Rusting is just the iron getting wet and stained, not a real chemical change.
items.random_wrong: Rusting is the iron slowly melting in the rain.
items.why: Because rusting is slow and undramatic, students class it as physical wear rather than the formation of a new compound.
Correct answer: Both oxygen and water. “Oxygen only” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: In the blast furnace, iron(III) oxide is reduced mainly by:
items.why: Correct answer: Carbon monoxide. “Oxygen” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Zinc protects iron sacrificially because zinc:
items.correct: Is more reactive and is oxidised in preference to the iron
items.random_wrong: Forms an alloy with the rust
items.why: It's Is more reactive and is oxidised in preference to the iron. “Is less reactive than iron” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
Correct answer: Loss of electrons. “Gain of electrons” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: What mass of iron is contained in 160 g of Fe₂O₃? (Ar: Fe = 56, O = 16; Mr of Fe₂O₃ = 160)
items.why: The real figure is 112 g. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
